title: Annual Review: RSE in the Golden Lab
section: 2️⃣ Let’s be honest, this is a lot…
text:
2️⃣ Let’s be honest, this is a lot…

Breadth: The scope of RSE/technical support/data management in academia is broad, spanning technical, educational, and operational domains. It requires learning new tools, deeply understanding disparate research project contexts, and building durable lab capabilities.
Novelty: Still a nascent field/territory within academia, with few established best practices & expert communities (USRSE < 8 years old). This means each lab is learning by doing, trial and error, and building from scratch ad infinitum.
Goal Misalignment: What pays bills? Papers. Not code. But what pays bills is often not what supports reproducibility, scalability, or even the research itself. This creates tension between short-term outputs and long-term infrastructure.

title: Annual Review: RSE in the Golden Lab
section: 2️⃣ Data-driven lab operations: FASRC and sacct
text:
2️⃣ Data-driven lab operations: FASRC and sacct
Core message: I implemented sacct-based metrics to quantify how the lab uses HPC resources and to support evidence-based planning.



Built reporting around Slurm activity for myself and other lab members.
Tracked jobs, CPU time, memory use, failures, and user/project-level utilization.
Created a basis for diagnosing bottlenecks and improving resource requests.
Shifted compute conversations from anecdotal to measurable.





Metric
Current value




Jobs submitted
[insert]


Completed jobs
[insert]


Failed jobs
[insert]


CPU-hours used
[insert]


Active users tracked
[insert]



Example chart to add: monthly jobs or CPU-hours by user/project.


Emphasize that the main accomplishment is the operational capability: the lab can now ask and answer questions about computing use. This supports allocation planning, debugging, onboarding, and future dashboards.

title: Annual Review: RSE in the Golden Lab
section: 3️⃣ Reproducibility and collaborative development: Git activity
text:
3️⃣ Reproducibility and collaborative development: Git activity
Core message: I helped normalize Git/GitHub as a scientific provenance system, not just a backup location for code.



Supported project organization around version-controlled workflows.
Encouraged issue-based work, branches, review, and clearer project histories.
Advanced practices using Quarto, R packages, targets, Python/R environments, and pipeline automation.
Reduced risk from undocumented analysis changes and one-person local workflows.





GitHub signal
Current value




Repositories supported
[insert]


Commits
[insert]


Issues opened/closed
[insert]


Pull requests
[insert]


Active contributors
[insert]



Interpretation: Git activity is a proxy for collaboration, provenance, and maintainability.


Do not overstate commits as productivity. Frame Git activity as evidence of research traceability and collaboration. Mention specific repositories or projects orally if useful.

title: Annual Review: RSE in the Golden Lab
section: 4️⃣ Data movement, documentation, and FAIR-aligned workflows
text:
4️⃣ Data movement, documentation, and FAIR-aligned workflows
Core message: I strengthened the lab’s ability to move, find, understand, and reuse data safely and efficiently.


Data movement

Supported Globus use for large and secure transfers.
Reduced reliance on fragile transfer methods such as local-only storage, email, or manual download chains.
Helped align data movement with shared infrastructure and project conventions.

Metrics to add

Transfers completed: [insert]
Data transferred: [insert]
Endpoints/users active: [insert]


Documentation

Created and refined guides for FASRC, project setup, Git, data locations, and workflow practices.
Made onboarding less dependent on repeated one-off explanations.
Advanced FAIR principles: findable, accessible, interoperable, reusable data and code.

Metrics to add

Pages created/updated: [insert]
Views or visits: [insert]
Top-used guide: [insert]



This slide pairs data movement with knowledge movement. The argument is that a lab cannot scale if data and operational knowledge live only in people’s heads.

title: Annual Review: RSE in the Golden Lab
text:
5️⃣ Strategic impact and next-year priorities
Core message: The biggest accomplishment is the emergence of an operational layer that makes lab research more scalable, reproducible, and measurable.


Impact this year

Made lab computing activity more visible.
Strengthened reproducible analysis practices.
Improved data transfer and documentation workflows.
Converted project-specific lessons into reusable lab infrastructure.
Helped students and collaborators work more independently and consistently.


Priorities for next year

Build a recurring RSE dashboard from sacct, GitHub, Globus, and docs analytics.
Standardize project templates for R, Python, Quarto, targets, and HPC workflows.
Formalize onboarding paths for students and staff.
Define data lifecycle conventions from intake to publication/archive.
Expand reproducible pipeline support for climate-health datasets.



Narrative close: RSE work turns individual technical support into durable lab capability.


Close by connecting accomplishments to institutional value: reproducibility, continuity, lower onboarding burden, better planning, and faster science.
